summary:
The Great Leap Forward was a campaign launched by Mao Zedong and the Chinese Communist Party from 1958 to 1962, aimed at rapidly transforming China from an agrarian society into a socialist industrial economy. The program combined forced agricultural collectivization with an ambitious drive to increase steel and iron production, largely through backyard furnaces operated by peasants across the country.

The campaign was a catastrophic failure on virtually every measure. Agricultural collectivization disrupted food production at its foundations: peasants were removed from farming to produce steel, crops rotted in fields for lack of harvesters, and local officials falsified production statistics to meet quotas, leading the government to export grain even as famine spread. The steel produced in backyard furnaces was largely useless for industrial purposes.

The resulting [[event:3347|Great Chinese Famine]] of 1959 to 1961 killed between 15 and 55 million people, making it the deadliest famine in human history. The true scale of the disaster was suppressed by the Chinese government for decades. Mao acknowledged failures in 1959 and formally ended the campaign in 1962, but he remained in power. The disaster briefly marginalized him politically and led to the rise of reformers such as Liu Shaoqi and Deng Xiaoping, who sought more pragmatic economic policies.

The Great Leap Forward is considered one of the greatest man-made disasters of the twentieth century. It deeply scarred Chinese society and created widespread trauma that was compounded by the subsequent [[event:2689|Cultural Revolution]]. Its legacy continues to influence debates about the relationship between political ideology and economic policy in China.
